content.assessment: A reconciled measurement architecture is well-suited to disentangling the demographic and diagnostic-practice components of rising SEN identification, which the record shows are genuinely contested: the 6.7%→7.7% and 3.9%→6.2% rises in identification (2010-2020) are attributed variably to diagnostic awareness, social disparities, and workforce shortages, and cross-national OECD evidence suggests diagnostic-practice change is a major driver of comparable rises elsewhere. A single longitudinal series could distinguish true cohort-driven prevalence change from artifacts of definitional or diagnostic shifts, which is foundational for any subsequent planning.
opportunities: Could incorporate regional disaggregation to map the pronounced North-South/East-West divide in child-population trends and disadvantage concentration, enabling geographically targeted planning
Natural policy variation points (2018, 2020 rule changes) offer quasi-experimental leverage for decomposing diagnostic-practice effects from demographic effects
strengths: Directly targets the demographic/diagnostic decomposition problem raised by contested attribution of SEN rise between diagnostic awareness and social/workforce factors
Would allow separation of locked-in cohort decline effects from identification-rate artifacts, improving the evidentiary basis for planning horizon decisions
threats: If reconciliation is not paired with regionally granular reporting, it may understate how differently cohort decline and identification interact across contracting rural areas versus high-disadvantage northeastern counties
weaknesses: A measurement framework alone does not address the underlying cohort contraction or spatial maldistribution; it is an epistemic rather than structural intervention
